item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image used in this article is cc-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as it comes from here under CC BY-SA 2.0.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Taking into account votes from deleted accounts and votes from accounts that are no longer members of the site, this article has a true rating of +2 higher than displayed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is incredibly bland. The description barely gives a feeling of being anomalous at all; if they built up the "urban predator" aspect more, I'd be all for it. Why limit it to pedestrian cross walks? Why not a section of sidewalk where kids regularly draw with chalk? Or the welcome mat to a small but popular neighborhood bakery? It can climb walls, so why not have it take the form of a corner of a building that people lean on if they're tired or want a smoke? What if it could go inside buildings?

This has so much potential to it. If this ever dips to deletion territory, I think I might want to take a crack at a rewrite.

item.title: Staff Post - Reversion Notice
item.content:encoded:
Reversions 19 and 20 were reverted back to Revision 18 due to many edits made out of the editor's scope. A missing it and a tense change were fine, but everything else were too major.

Please read the Rules on editing. If you're not sure if a change is correct, message the author.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm kicking this a +1. It may not be the scariest thing I've read, but I have no doubt I'll look at crosswalks (especially crosswalks over speed bumps like we have where I live) with a bit more caution.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hello there! I read through this article, I did understand the article and the tone of it sounded fine. I personally would be scared if I had to cross the street with this thing lurking unseen. I guess the image wasn't really addressed to an extent really in my own biased opinion as you stated that it is a flatworm that looks like something harmless. I guess for description and a realization that maybe this is sorta the reason why the crosswalk is sometimes not used by people around. I'm kinda iffy on the image only but…..

With all due respect, +1

item.title: Re: Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Honestly, I'm back and re-reading it and it doesn't inspire me either. I'll happily surrender it to someone for a rewrite if there is a volunteer.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Overall it comes off as Meh. Perhaps a recovery log, or something might give it some meat, but I just don't see it. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Given the strictness of the containment procedures, I feel this should definitely be a Euclid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I enjoy it, but the scare quotes feel like cop-outs.

item.title: Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
This article is up for review. Check it out, we feel it needs more attention.

This feels… really bland to me. Like, I think the idea of some monster that imitates crosswalks and rugs and other stuff like that could be a really cool base idea, but this never grows beyond that idea. The reproduction thing at the end doesn't really do all that much to add to the idea, and isn't really all that well-written to boot. I think if this idea could be built on into something that differentiates it from a D&D monster, it could be cool, but as it stands I'm rather bored by it. Picture is pretty decent though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It will grow in area at a rate of approximately 1 cm2 per day with consistent feeding, scaling exponentially if supplied with more or less than its minimum necessary diet.

Why exponentially? Linearly would make more sense (unless how it grows is part of is anomalous behavior).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nitpick away - I'm a engineer (in-training), not a biologist, so the more such corrections offered, the better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
More biologist nit-picks!

Firstly, I know I suggested aestivate earlier, but even that term is bugging me. See, "aestivate" is a verb and you're using it as an adjective to describe a state. "Torpid" actually is an adjective, so it'd fit better as a part of speech. Plus, torpid is more general; aestivation has a specific biological/metabolic role while becoming torpid could be caused by many factors and is therefore more mysteeeerious *cue creepy music*

On a flatworm, the pharynx serves as both the throat and mouth - it's not really an opening to a throat, nor do turbellarians have a separate opening that might be called a throat or mouth. Basically the upper digestive tract is a tube that can be moved around outside the body, like eating through a bendy straw. So you could just say "SCP-1927's anterior end is distinguished only by the presence of a pharynx, a tube-like organ used both to capture and to ingest prey," or something similar. You'll also need to make similar adjustments to the description of the mode of attack in the paragraph on hunting tactics.

Finally, I feel the use of quotes around "hunts" is excessive. Given the information that follows, it's quite clear this is a hunting tactic. You could say something like "SCP-1927 has been observed to spread itself on a horizontal surface…"

I'm now upvoting because of the likeness to a giant flatworm. You added a lot of biological detail to this since I last read it and I think the added rigor helps!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this one actually. Quaint, simple, worthy of containment. Not bad. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I assumed that. It's still kind of funny. Imagine a predator that advertised what it was going to do to you…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …that wasn't intentional, I swear.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this enough for an upvote. Also, it's cute that the signs on the pole look like before and after shots.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The floor is lava worms trying to eat us. I don't know, I kind of like it for some reason. Seems like a thing a bunch of rapscallions would spread rumors about, and then proceed to be eaten by.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've done my best to squeeze out the D&Dness in this latest version, but by now the damage has been done…and it's still a Thing What Eats People, there's no escaping that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Having never played DnD, I can say that I like this one enough to upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And semi-significant revisions made, taking Doctor Ether's suggestion and running with it. It's now officially a giant flatworm-like creature (currently taking advice on how it maintains respiration with its current thickness), and instead of lurking like a Trapper, its hunting technique is now more like that of a moray eel.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Too much or too little? If I made it too thin, it wouldn't make sense as an active predator - you could just punch/kick your way free before it could crush you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't know why, but for some reason it being about a foot thick bugs me…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Copy+replacement errors - it was originally 'sessile', I forgot to change the a/n when updating to the correct word.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It reads to me like nothing interesting- just another thing that looks normal but really kills you.

it springs into motion with a speed uncharacteristic of its typical slow undulations

This is rather tone breaking to me. It doesn't help that I typically only see the word 'undulations' in badly done gay erotica.

a aestivative state

I do believe you are missing an 'an'.

ceasing and entering a aestivative state

…How did you make the same mistake again? It just is jarring to read.

But, yeah, it just seems to me like something that can look like things but really kills you. Meeeeeh.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Actually, those are pretty awesome, and the biology is already pretty similar - flat, hermaphroditic, predatory. I'd just need to change the hunting pattern - rather than envelop something on top of it, it lunges to swallow and crush something right in front of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hey, here's an idea. Make it a flatworm. They aren't always parasitic, and they have this crazy method of eating called the pharynx. It even hunts in a similar manner to this thing at times. Look 'em up, they're awesome.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Welcome to suggestions - I'm personally of the feel that the capture addendum is either weak or completely unnecessary, but not sure which.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Rerating to an upvote, but I can't help but feel some sections need rewording.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I see what you did there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like to think I has the goodest grammar, point em out for me?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is pretty good. Just a few grammar errors here and there, but otherwise solid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Now that you remind me, I read a Head Injury Theater article about that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The purpose was more intended as a 'get off the walls and onto the floor where we can see you' deterrent, since it can't melt its way through the walls anyways. I'll switch the electric shock description and see if that helps.

As for the rest…I guess that's why living SCPs are so hard to write. I had given it the ability to grow 'hair' as a vestigal trait of before there were fixed paths and it lived in fields or grassy areas, whereas now it colors itself like paths and crosswalks, but it's evidentally a fine line between weird and unbelievable. I landed three SCPs in a row successfully, I guess I'm due for a failure.

I did establish that it reproduces by budding…maybe some way to imply it's not the only one of its kind out there? Any zebra crossing or clean grassy field could eat an unsuspecting jogger?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well. A voltage is good, just reword it. You don't want a sustained current, you want a high voltage, perhaps a few kV with a low current to shock it and persuade it from leaving.
In such an application , beyond designing the system we prob'ly don't give that much of a damn about current involved as it's gonna be low.

Now, as for D&D monster.. that's more a category where the thing is weird, yes, but it isn't particularly disturbing, nor is it interesting enough, nor realistic enough to actually believe it exists.. it's the pattern of thought that "Hey, the thing I want to see here is a stat block"
That said, there are monsters just like this, someone mentioned Trapper, and well, Mimics conceal themselves as smaller objects, but that's all besides.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It was intended to mean dormant, since 'comatose' or 'hibernating' wouldn't apply to a creature without detectable brain activity. If there's a more appropriate word to use, I can swap it in.

EDIT: Apparently that is aestivation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: D&D has a set of monsters that basically lets the DM turn AN ENTIRE ROOM and all its furnishings into monsters that want to eat the party.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I flip-flopped a few times on that - I guess I should have used amperage instead of volts?

As for being a 'D&D monster'…a good portion of the stuff on this site could be a 'D&D monster' or a 'D&D cursed magic item'. I can accept the idea being bland/boring/lame, but being downvoted because Gary Gygax came up with a similar idea is frustrating, to say the least.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: By "sessile" do you mean fixed to one place and not moving? Or do you mean aestivating or otherwise dormant?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
300 V is a potential difference, not a current. Nor is it enough to specify.
That said, the brunt of the problem with this one is that this is a DnD monster more so than an SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This isn't all that bad, but the concept is a little dull. Very nice job fleshing out the biology of this thing, but I'm afraid it's not enough to warrant an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You mean SCP-075? I'm not really sure how they're comparable…075 attacks things by pouncing on them and dissolving them, this is an ambush predator that constricts its prey, the acid is just for concealment. I guess it could be a weak acid instead of a strong one, since it doesn't use the stuff to hunt, but 'has acid' isn't really an overlapping concept.

Also, pic shrunk and replaced. Any better?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Usually they don't black out single digits. Just Saiyan. Other than that, this is. Okay. There's a snail that does the dissolving thing, though, so you may want to make it a tad less overpowered. Maybe have it burrow or something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Also, that pic is 1000x800 pixels and ~184kB in size. It might just be my monitor, but there are some awful jaggedys all through the pic due to scale compression. Try resizing it smaller and uploading that version.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…really?

Damn, D&D takes all the good monsters. Hopefully people will believe that wasn't intentional. The inspiration was just looking at a crosswalk on the way to class today and wondering if it might try to eat me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Trapper_(Dungeons_%26_Dragons)

Right?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Crap. I thought i Find+Replaced those…fixing.

Okay, the XXXX's are gone. The title is correct, though - SCP-1927 like it should be.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: SCP-XXXX and page name is Scp 1927

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I didn't get further than the title and all the SCP-XXXX's. Please fix.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
And back on the horse (I hope) with another 'what if completely innocuous everday thing actually wanted to kill you'.

Thanks to GrandEnder and SwamplessThing in chat for proofreading and critiquing.
